title: Financial institutions, microprudential policy, financial markets and payments
summary: This is the ECB's research agenda page outlining areas of scholarly interest regarding financial institutions, microprudential policy, financial markets, and payments. It is not an actual policy document, data release, or market-moving announcement. The page lists research themes including central bank liquidity and collateral policies, bank supervision architecture, and fintech/digital currencies without providing specific policy decisions, numbers, or forecasts.
key_points: This is a research agenda/index page listing ECB research interests, not a policy statement or data release
Research areas include: central bank liquidity and collateral policies impact on financial markets
Research areas include: optimal design of supervisory architecture, including centralisation effects and climate change implications
Research areas include: fintechs, bigtechs effects on banking industry and potential effects on financial stability
Research areas include: design and benefits of central bank digital currencies for the financial system
No specific policy decisions, rate decisions, or quantitative targets are presented on this page
why_it_matters: This page has no direct market impact as it contains only a research agenda rather than actual policy or data. It may be useful for understanding the ECB's analytical priorities but provides no actionable information for traders or investors.

issues_found: Source is a listing page (index.en.html) - non-archivable per CORE RULE 6
No published_at date provided in source metadata
Source contains only broad research topic headings, no specific policy content, data, or forecasts
event_type is 'unknown' - but listing pages should not be archived regardless of event type
No market-moving information or macro catalysts present

notes: This source is a research agenda listing page from the ECB website. Per CORE RULE 6, listing pages should be treated as non-archiveable. The page contains no specific policy decisions, data releases, speeches, or any content that would constitute a macro catalyst. It merely outlines broad research areas of interest (liquidity policies, bank supervision, fintech, digital currencies) without providing concrete findings, numbers, or forecasts. Source confidence is very low due to lack of actionable content.
